title: Externalities & public goods policy
summary:
## The big idea Unit 6 diagnosed the disease: externalities and public goods push markets away from the social optimum (**MSB = MSC**). This chapter is the pharmacy — taxes, permits, regulation, subsidies, state provision — and, just as importantly, the honest label on every bottle: *may cause government failure*. Top answers know several tools, draw their diagrams, and weigh them against each other. ## Correcting negative externalities ### Pigouvian taxes — make the polluter pay Set an indirect tax **equal to the external cost**, so the producer's private cost rises to the true social cost — the externality is **internalised**:
**What each part means:** untaxed, the market sits at **Qmkt** (grey dot), where MPB = MPC — over-producing because the pollution cost is ignored. A tax equal to the **orange arrow** (the external cost per unit) lifts the firm's cost curve from **MPC** up to the dashed **MSC** line. The market now settles at **Qopt** (orange dot), where MSB = MSC — the social optimum. The welfare-loss triangle from Unit 6 is eliminated, and the government collects revenue along the way.
*Pros:* market-based (leaves firms free to respond), raises **revenue** (usable for clean-up or compensation), 'polluter pays' feels fair, and it creates a *continuous* incentive to cut pollution. *Cons:* the killer is the **information problem** — nobody can measure the external cost precisely, so the 'right' rate is a guess; **inelastic demand** means little quantity change; it can be **regressive**; and firms may relocate ('pollution havens') or evade. ### Tradable pollution permits — cap and trade Instead of pricing pollution, **cap its quantity**: the government fixes total allowed emissions, issues that many **permits**, and lets firms **trade** them. Clean firms sell spare permits (profit from cutting emissions); dirty firms must buy (pollution becomes a cost either way). *Pros:* **certainty about the total quantity** of pollution (a tax leaves quantity uncertain; permits leave *price* uncertain — the neatest compare-and-contrast in the topic); abatement happens where it's *cheapest*; the market discovers the price. The **EU Emissions Trading Scheme** is the giant example. *Cons:* set the cap too generously and permits are nearly free (the ETS's early embarrassment); monitoring and verification cost money; grandfathered permits can gift windfalls to polluters; volatile permit prices deter investment. ### Regulation and bans Emission limits, technology standards, outright bans (leaded petrol, CFCs). *Pros:* simple, certain, fast — essential where harm is catastrophic. *Cons:* blunt (same rule regardless of each firm's abatement cost — expensive overall), **no incentive to cut below the limit**, enforcement costs, capture risk. ## Encouraging positive externalities and merit goods The mirror-image toolkit: **subsidies** (shift MPC down, or effectively raise consumption toward the MSB = MSC optimum), **direct state provision** (education, vaccination programmes), **information campaigns** and compulsion (school attendance laws). Costs and caveats as in AS Unit 3: budget opportunity cost, information problems, provision may be x-inefficient. ## Public goods — when only provision works **Non-excludable + non-rival** → **free riders** → complete market failure: private supply barely exists, whatever the price mechanism does. The standard answer is **state provision funded by taxation**, with output chosen by comparing total social benefit and cost (cost–benefit analysis, since there is no market price to read). Two refinements: technology can make some goods excludable (toll roads, encrypted broadcasts — turning them into 'quasi-public' goods a market *can* supply), and provision can be state-funded but privately *produced* (contracted-out road building). ## The Coase theorem — does government even need to act? Coase's insight: if **property rights are clearly assigned** and **bargaining costs are low**, the affected parties can negotiate their own solution — the polluter pays the victims (or the victims pay the polluter to stop), and the outcome is efficient *whoever* holds the right. A beekeeper and an orchard owner don't need a ministry. **Why it often fails in practice:** millions of affected parties (climate change), unclear rights (who owns the atmosphere?), and high transaction costs. Still — a first-class evaluation point: *the case for intervention rests on bargaining being impossible*. ## Government failure — the constant caveat Every tool above can misfire: wrong tax rate or cap (imperfect information), unintended consequences (pollution relocates; a bin tax breeds fly-tipping), administration and enforcement costs, **regulatory capture**, and political horizons shorter than environmental ones. The mature conclusion is usually a **package** — e.g. a carbon price *plus* standards *plus* R&D subsidies — sized against the seriousness of the failure.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Target: move output to **MSB = MSC** without triggering government failure. - **Pigouvian tax** = external cost → MPC rises to MSC; revenue; but valuation guesswork, inelastic demand, regressive. - **Permits (cap-and-trade)**: quantity certain, least-cost abatement, price uncertain — mirror image of a tax (price certain, quantity uncertain). - **Regulation**: certain and fast, but blunt, costly to enforce, no incentive beyond the limit. - Positive externalities/merit goods: subsidy, provision, information, compulsion. - **Public goods**: free-rider → state provision via taxes; CBA decides quantity; some goods can be made excludable. - **Coase**: clear property rights + low transaction costs → private bargains fix externalities — intervention isn't always needed. - Always weigh **market failure vs government failure**; packages beat single tools.

keyTerms.definition: A tax set equal to the external cost of a good, shifting MPC up to MSC to internalise the externality.
A cap-and-trade system where firms buy and sell rights to emit within a fixed total cap.
Making producers/consumers bear the full social cost or benefit of their actions.
Non-payers consuming a non-excludable good, so markets under-provide it.
The idea that clear property rights and low bargaining costs let private parties resolve externalities without government.
When intervention creates a net welfare loss through distortions, costs or unintended effects.

diagrams: **Pigouvian tax:** on a negative-production-externality diagram, show the tax shifting MPC up to MSC so output falls to the social optimum; mark the revenue and the removed welfare loss.
**Subsidy for a positive externality:** shift MPB (or MPC) to reach the MSB = MSC optimum for a merit good.
**Tradable permits:** a vertical (fixed) supply of permits and a demand for them setting a permit price; show the incentive to abate.
tips: For each policy give the **diagram + a balanced evaluation** (pros, cons, government failure) — don't just list tools.
Stress the **information problem**: valuing the externality to set the ‘right’ tax/cap is very difficult.
Compare **taxes vs permits**: taxes fix the price of pollution, permits fix the *quantity* — a sophisticated point.
Use the **Coase theorem** and **property rights** as an evaluation of whether government action is even needed.
Bring in real context (**Sweden's carbon tax, the EU Emissions Trading Scheme, London's congestion charge, Indonesia's fuel-subsidy reform**) for application marks.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear coverage of externality policies and permits.

channels.why: Strong on tax vs permit evaluation.

keyConcepts.title: Correcting externalities with taxes and subsidies

revisionNotes:
# Externality & public-goods policy — quick revision

**Negative externalities**
- **Pigouvian tax** = external cost: MPC → MSC, output → Qopt, revenue raised.
  - Weaknesses: valuation (information problem), inelastic demand, regressive, relocation/evasion.
- **Tradable permits**: cap total emissions, let firms trade.
  - Quantity certainty + least-cost abatement + market price discovery (EU ETS).
  - Weaknesses: cap-setting, monitoring, windfalls, price volatility.
  - **Tax fixes the price of pollution; permits fix the quantity** — star comparison.
- **Regulation/bans**: certain, fast, right for catastrophic harms; blunt, enforcement cost, no incentive below the limit.

**Positive externalities / merit goods** — subsidies, direct provision, information, compulsion (with budget + info caveats).

**Public goods**
- Non-excludable + non-rival → free riders → missing market → **state provision from taxes**; quantity via cost–benefit analysis.
- Quasi-public: tolls/encryption can restore excludability; provision ≠ production (contracting out).

**Coase theorem** — clear property rights + low transaction costs → private bargaining reaches efficiency without government; fails with many parties/unclear rights (climate).

**Government failure checklist** — wrong rate/cap, unintended consequences, admin costs, capture, short-termism.

**Exam habit** — diagram per policy + explicit comparison + package-style judgement.
questions:
# Practice questions — Externalities & public goods policy **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** To fully correct a negative production externality, a Pigouvian tax should be set equal to: - A — the firm's marginal private cost - B — the external cost per unit at the socially optimal output - C — the firm's total revenue - D — the market price of the good
▸ Show answer

B. The tax must close the gap between MPC and MSC — i.e. equal the marginal external cost — so the market settles where MSB = MSC.

**2.** Compared with a pollution tax, a scheme of tradable pollution permits provides more certainty about: - A — the price polluters will pay - B — the total quantity of pollution - C — government revenue - D — firms' profits
▸ Show answer

B. The cap fixes the quantity of emissions; the permit price then floats. A tax does the reverse — price certain, quantity uncertain.

**3.** Private markets fail to supply pure public goods mainly because: - A — production costs are too high - B — non-excludability allows free riders to consume without paying - C — demand for them is too low - D — they create negative externalities
▸ Show answer

B. If you can't exclude non-payers, you can't charge — so no revenue, no private supply, however valuable the good.

**4.** According to the Coase theorem, an externality can be corrected without government intervention provided that: - A — the externality is small - B — property rights are clearly defined and bargaining costs are low - C — the government owns the resource - D — all firms are perfectly competitive
▸ Show answer

B. With clear rights and cheap bargaining, the parties negotiate an efficient outcome themselves — whoever holds the rights.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A country generating most electricity from coal must cut carbon emissions 30% by 2035. Officials are split between a carbon tax of \$30 per tonne and a cap-and-trade scheme. Colleagues studying the EU's Emissions Trading Scheme note that its early caps were so generous that permit prices collapsed, but that its tightened cap later drove substantial switching to renewables. Industry warns either policy will raise electricity bills, hitting poor households hardest. **(a)** Define 'internalising an externality'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with a diagram, how the \$30 carbon tax would reduce emissions. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** advantages of cap-and-trade over the carbon tax for meeting the 30% target. **[6]** **(d)** Assess which policy the government should choose. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Making producers/consumers bear the full social cost (or benefit) of their actions, e.g. via a tax equal to the external cost. [1+1]

(b) Tax shifts MPC up toward MSC → electricity price rises, quantity (and coal use) falls toward Qopt; continuous incentive to switch fuels. Diagram as in Fig 1. [4]

(c) (i) Quantity certainty: the cap can be set exactly on the 30% path, whereas a tax's emission outcome is uncertain; (ii) least-cost abatement: firms with cheap abatement cut most and sell permits — the target is met at minimum total cost. (Also creditable: automatic tightening, revenue from auctioning.) [3+3]

(d) Tax: predictable price → investment planning; simpler; revenue can offset bills for the poor (address regressivity). Permits: hits the target with certainty but risks the EU's early mistake (loose cap, volatile price); auctioning + a price floor mitigates. Either choice scores with: distributional fix (rebates), EU-evidence use, and a clear justified conclusion. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why a pure public good will not be provided by the free market. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether direct government provision is always the best response to this failure. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Non-excludability → free-rider problem → no revenue → missing market; non-rivalry means MC of another user is zero, so charging is also inefficient. Both characteristics explained.

(b) For provision: only taxation defeats free riding; universal access. Against 'always': quasi-public goods can be made excludable (tolls, encryption); provision can be state-funded but privately produced; CBA needed to avoid over-provision; government failure (cost overruns, political projects). Judgement: provision for pure cases, mixed solutions elsewhere.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain how a system of tradable pollution permits works to reduce emissions. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether pollution is better tackled by taxes, permits or regulation. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Cap → permits issued/auctioned → trading → pollution priced → abatement concentrated where cheapest; clean firms profit from selling.

(b) Tax: price certainty, revenue, but quantity uncertain + valuation problem. Permits: quantity certainty, least-cost, but volatile prices + cap-setting. Regulation: speed and certainty for severe/local harms, but blunt and incentive-free. Judgement: match tool to problem (catastrophic → ban; diffuse CO₂ → price mechanism); hybrid packages (cap with price floor) often dominate; government failure applies to all.
